description: Implement all 9 planned OS milestones: serial output, IDT, PIT timer, keyboard, virtual memory, scheduler, userspace, filesystem, and SMP

userStories.title: Serial output (COM1) — port I/O stubs and TX
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the kernel to output text to COM1 so that I can view logs in the QEMU terminal (-serial stdio) without needing a GUI.
acceptanceCriteria: Add outb(port, val) and inb(port) assembly stubs to src/stubs.S for x86 port I/O
Create src/serial.go: initialize COM1 (0x3F8) at 115200 baud 8N1, implement serialPutChar and serialPrint
Update src/main.go: VGA line 0 shows 'Serial: OK' and same message sent to COM1
make build succeeds with no linker errors
make run-kernel shows serial output on the terminal via -serial stdio
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: First milestone. outb/inb stubs are reused by all subsequent hardware milestones (PIC, PIT, keyboard, APIC).

userStories.title: IDT structure and lidt — define 256-entry IDT in Go, load with assembly
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a 256-entry Interrupt Descriptor Table loaded into the CPU so that exception and IRQ vectors can be dispatched.
acceptanceCriteria: Create src/idt.go: define IDTEntry struct (16 bytes per entry), idtTable [256]IDTEntry array, setGate helper
Add lidt assembly stub to src/stubs.S that loads the IDT register from a Go-provided pointer
Call idtInit() from main() to populate all 256 entries with a default handler and load via lidt
VGA shows 'IDT: loaded, 256 entries'
make build succeeds
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Does NOT enable interrupts yet. ISR stubs and PIC remap come in the next stories.

userStories.title: ISR stubs and interrupt dispatcher — assembly entry points + Go handler table
userStories.description: As a developer, I want assembly ISR entry stubs that save registers and call a Go-side dispatcher so that interrupt handlers can be written in Go.
acceptanceCriteria: Create src/isr.S: 256 ISR stub entry points using macros (push error code / dummy, push vector number, save registers, call go_interrupt_handler, restore, iretq)
Create or extend src/idt.go: register each ISR stub address in the IDT entries
Implement go_interrupt_handler(vector uint64) in Go as a table-driven dispatcher
Handle vector 0 (division by zero) as test: display 'Exception: #DE' on VGA
Update Makefile to assemble src/isr.S if it is a new file
make build succeeds
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-002. Interrupts still disabled (no sti yet). PIC remap comes next.

userStories.title: PIC remap and enable interrupts — remap 8259A, unmask IRQs, sti
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the PIC remapped so hardware IRQs 0-15 map to vectors 32-47 and interrupts are enabled.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement picRemap() in Go using outb: remap master PIC to vectors 32-39, slave to 40-47
Implement picSendEOI(irq) in Go
Add sti assembly stub to src/stubs.S
Call picRemap() then sti from main() after IDT is loaded
VGA shows 'Interrupts: enabled'
Kernel runs for several seconds without triple-fault after sti
make build succeeds
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-002, US-003. After this, hardware IRQs (timer, keyboard) can be handled.

userStories.title: PIT timer — program channel 0 at 100 Hz, count ticks
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a hardware timer generating periodic interrupts so the kernel has a notion of time.
acceptanceCriteria: Program PIT channel 0 to fire IRQ0 at ~100 Hz (10ms interval) using outb
Register IRQ0 handler (vector 32) that increments a global tick counter and sends EOI
VGA shows 'Timer: NNNN ticks' updated after a short spin-wait in main()
Serial output also logs tick count
make build succeeds; kernel runs without triple-fault for at least several seconds
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-004 (PIC remap + sti). Timer tick is used later for preemptive scheduling.

userStories.title: PS/2 keyboard driver — IRQ1 handler, scancode-to-ASCII, VGA echo
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the kernel to read keyboard input so the user can interact with the OS.
acceptanceCriteria: Register IRQ1 handler (vector 33) that reads scancode from port 0x60 and sends EOI
Implement minimal scancode set 1 to ASCII mapping (letters a-z, digits 0-9, space, enter, backspace)
Display typed characters on a dedicated VGA line (simple echo buffer)
VGA shows 'Keyboard: ready' and echoes keystrokes below
Serial output logs each keypress
make build succeeds; typing in QEMU produces visible output on VGA
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-004 (PIC remap + sti). After this the kernel has interactive I/O.

userStories.title: Virtual memory — page fault handler, mapPage/unmapPage in Go
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the kernel to manage virtual memory with 4 KiB page granularity so page faults are handled and pages can be dynamically mapped.
acceptanceCriteria: Register page fault handler (vector 14) that reads CR2 (faulting address) and error code, displays on VGA
Add assembly stub to read CR2 register
Implement mapPage(vaddr, paddr, flags uintptr) in Go: walk/create 4-level page table entries for 4 KiB pages
Implement unmapPage(vaddr uintptr) in Go: clear the PTE and flush TLB (invlpg assembly stub)
Demo in main(): map a fresh 4 KiB page at a high address, write to it, read back, unmap, show success
VGA shows 'VM: map/unmap OK'
make build succeeds; no triple-fault during map/unmap
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-002 (IDT for page fault). Existing 2 MiB identity map remains; this adds 4 KiB granularity for new mappings.

userStories.title: Scheduler — task struct, context switch assembly, round-robin with PIT preemption
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a preemptive round-robin scheduler so multiple tasks run concurrently.
acceptanceCriteria: Define Task struct in Go: ID, stack pointer, state (running/ready/blocked), entry function pointer
Implement context switch in assembly: save callee-saved registers + RSP to current task, restore from next task
Implement schedule() in Go: round-robin picks the next ready task
Hook PIT IRQ0 handler to call schedule() for preemptive switching
Create 2-3 demo tasks that each write to different VGA lines in a loop
VGA shows interleaved output from multiple tasks
Serial logs task switches
make build succeeds; tasks switch without triple-fault
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-005 (PIT timer). Context switch assembly is the critical piece.

userStories.title: Userspace — TSS, GDT Ring 3 segments, iretq to user mode, minimal syscall
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the kernel to run a user-mode function in Ring 3 with a syscall interface back to the kernel.
acceptanceCriteria: Set up a TSS with RSP0 pointing to the kernel stack for privilege transitions
Add ltr assembly stub to load the Task Register
Add Ring 3 code and data segments (DPL=3) to the GDT
Implement jump-to-Ring-3 via iretq (assembly): push SS, RSP, RFLAGS, CS, RIP for user mode
Implement syscall entry in assembly: save user state, switch to kernel stack, call Go syscall handler, sysret back
User function calls a 'print' syscall that writes to VGA via the kernel
VGA shows 'User: Hello from Ring 3!'
make build succeeds; user program runs without GP fault
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-007 (VM for user address space) and US-008 (scheduler for task management). Most assembly-heavy milestone.

userStories.title: In-memory filesystem — flat directory with create/write/read/list in Go
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a minimal in-memory filesystem so the kernel can create, read, and write named files.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement src/fs.go: FileSystem struct with flat directory of fixed-size entries
Support Create(name string), Write(name string, data []byte), Read(name string) []byte, List() []string
Demo in main(): create 'hello.txt', write 'hello world', read back and verify, list directory
VGA shows 'FS: create/write/read OK' and lists file names
Serial logs filesystem operations
make build succeeds
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Pure Go implementation, no assembly needed. Kernel-only API (no userspace syscall integration in this story).

userStories.title: SMP — discover APs, real-mode trampoline, INIT-SIPI-SIPI, multi-core boot
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the kernel to boot all CPU cores so multi-core execution is demonstrated.
acceptanceCriteria: Detect AP count (hardcode for QEMU or parse ACPI MADT if feasible)
Write a 16-bit real-mode AP trampoline in assembly placed below 1 MiB (page-aligned)
Implement INIT-SIPI-SIPI sequence using Local APIC MMIO writes
Each AP transitions from real mode to long mode and atomically increments a shared counter
BSP spin-waits for APs, then displays core count on VGA
VGA shows 'SMP: N cores online'
Serial logs each AP coming online
make build succeeds; make run-kernel with -smp 4 shows 4 cores
All code comments in English
userStories.notes: Depends on US-004 (IDT/APIC). Most complex milestone. AP trampoline is 16-bit real-mode assembly that must be relocated below 1 MiB.
